card.intuition: k slot machines (arms) with unknown reward distributions. Goal: maximise cumulative reward by balancing exploration (trying new arms) vs exploitation (pulling the best-known arm). UCB1: pull arm i* = argmax[Q̄ᵢ + √(2·ln t / nᵢ)]. Regret = T·μ* − Σ rewards; UCB1 achieves O(k·log T) regret — logarithmic, nearly optimal.
card.analogy: Testing k restaurants in a new city: you could always go to your current favourite (exploit) or try a place you haven't visited much (explore). UCB1 adds a 'curiosity bonus' — the less you've tried a restaurant, the more bonus it gets — ensuring you adequately explore before committing.
card.time_complexity: O(k) per round to compute UCB scores
when_to_use: Online A/B testing: which variant to serve each user
Clinical trials: adaptive allocation to better treatments
Ad selection, recommendation systems, hyperparameter search
gotchas: Assumes stationary reward distributions — use sliding window or discounted UCB for non-stationary
ε-greedy is simpler but has O(k·T) regret vs UCB's O(k·log T)
Thompson sampling (Bayesian) often outperforms UCB empirically despite similar theoretical guarantees

hints: Select the arm with the highest UCB score using argmax over the scores list.
exercise.test_code:
import random
random.seed(42)

# Arm 2 has best mean reward=0.9, others 0.1
means = [0.1, 0.1, 0.9]
bandit = UCB1Bandit(k=3)

for _ in range(500):
    arm = bandit.select()
    reward = random.gauss(means[arm], 0.1)
    bandit.update(arm, reward)

# Best arm should be pulled the most
assert bandit.counts[2] > bandit.counts[0]
assert bandit.counts[2] > bandit.counts[1]
assert bandit.values[2] > 0.7, f'Best arm value: {bandit.values[2]:.3f}'
print('PASS')
